# Specification Manual

This document defines how specifications are authored and maintained in TPL-App.

For test execution, reports, and suggestion handling, see `doc/test-manual.qmd`.

## Scope of This Manual

- Keep specifications human-readable in Markdown
- Keep them in a separate global location
- Define clear, stable expected behavior
- Keep specs independent from generated run artifacts

## Where Specification Files Live

- Server-only test specs: `spec/testing/server/*.md`
- Client-server-wide test specs: `spec/testing/client-server/*.md`
- Conceptual consistency specs: `spec/testing/conceptual/*.md`
- Business/domain requirement specifications: `spec/requirements/*.md`
- Formal architecture specs: `spec/architecture/*`

Test specs are plain Markdown documents with structured `spec-case` blocks.

Business requirement specs in `spec/requirements/*.md` are maintained as plain Markdown.

## Domain Concept Model (PlantUML)

TPL-App also keeps intent-level conceptual domain specification artifacts in:

- `spec/domain/*.puml`

These files describe business concepts and conceptual relationships independently from direct database implementation details.

### Artifacts and purpose

- `spec/domain/gen-domain.puml`: integrated overview across all architecture domains
- `spec/domain/gen-paths-domain.puml`: path/scenario view (`P1..P6`) for cross-domain journeys
- `spec/domain/BASE-domain.puml`: BASE foundations (identity/reference/scheduler/translation)
- `spec/domain/CROSS-domain.puml`: CROSS cross-cutting runtime concerns (tasks/information/favorites)
- `spec/domain/pat-domain.puml`: PAT patient and medical-values concepts
- `spec/domain/tplk-domain.puml`: TPLK episode/coordination/procurement concepts
- `spec/domain/koll-domain.puml`: KOLL colloquium and agenda relationships
- `spec/domain/ltpl-domain.puml`: LTPL domain boundary/status in current model
- `spec/domain/stcs-domain.puml`: STCS domain boundary/status in current model
- `spec/domain/domain-sync-mapping.json`: alias/exception config for conceptual-vs-model checks

### How to work with these files

- Source of truth is the `.puml` text files in `spec/domain/`
- Edit manually in the editor
- Render/preview visually via PlantUML
- Keep concept names aligned with business language (not DB naming)

Render example:

```{bash}
plantuml spec/domain/gen-domain.puml
```

### Automated conceptual/model sync check

Use the checker to verify that conceptual entities/relations still match SQLAlchemy model intent:

```{bash}
cd /Users/stephan/Workspace/TPL-App/backend
source .venv/bin/activate
cd /Users/stephan/Workspace/TPL-App
python -m qa.spec_tools.check_domain_sync
```

Default check inputs:

- diagram: `spec/domain/gen-domain.puml`
- mapping config: `spec/domain/domain-sync-mapping.json`

If the checker fails, update the conceptual diagrams and/or mapping config in the same change set as the model change.

### Update rules (required)

Update affected `spec/domain/*.puml` files in the same change set when any of these change:

- a conceptual entity is added/removed/renamed
- a conceptual relationship or cardinality changes
- a key domain invariant or ownership intent changes
- a primary path in `gen-paths-domain.puml` changes (`P1..P6`)

Usually no conceptual diagram update is required for implementation-only technical changes (for example index changes or internal column refactors) when concept intent is unchanged.

### Pull request checklist for domain changes

- identify impacted conceptual diagram(s)
- update diagram(s) and path view if needed
- mention impacted path IDs (`P1..P6`) in PR notes when behavior/journey changes

## Architecture Specification

TPL-App keeps formal architecture artifacts in:

- `spec/architecture/context.puml`
- `spec/architecture/container.puml`
- `spec/architecture/backend-components.puml`
- `spec/architecture/dependency-rules.json`

These artifacts define architecture intent and machine-checkable dependency constraints.

### Automated architecture dependency check

Run:

```{bash}
cd /Users/stephan/Workspace/TPL-App/backend
source .venv/bin/activate
cd /Users/stephan/Workspace/TPL-App
python -m qa.spec_tools.check_architecture_dependencies
```

The checker validates backend imports against `spec/architecture/dependency-rules.json`.

## Requirement Specification Standard

All requirement specification documents under `spec/requirements/` must follow the same hierarchy:

1. Feature
2. Use Case
3. Story
4. Item

Use Case, Story, and Item levels should each contain exactly one status label.

Allowed labels:

- `Spec`: ongoing specification
- `Ready`: ready for implementation
- `Impl`: currently being implemented
- `QA`: implementation done, QA test creation outstanding
- `Done`: fully completed

Feature hub document pattern:

- `spec/requirements/<LABEL>-<Feature-Name>.md` (for example `spec/requirements/TPLK-Koordination-Transplantation.md`)

Meta/template documents:

- Spec-system definition: `spec/requirements/GEN-Spec-Spezifikation.md`
- Architecture baseline: `spec/requirements/GEN-Architektur-Spezifikation.md`
- Architecture review backlog: `spec/requirements/GEN-Architektur-Backlog.md`
- Short starter template for new specs: `spec/requirements/TEMPLATE-Spezifikation.md`

### Label format (low-overhead Markdown)

Business requirement specifications in `spec/requirements/` use plain Markdown status tokens:

Apply labels directly in Use Case/Story/Item headings (without a dash before the status):

```md
# Feature [TPLK]: Koordination Transplantation
# Feature-of [TPLK]: Koordination Transplantation
## Use Case: <Name> [Ready]
### Story: <Name> [Impl]
```

Item level should use the same heading-inline style:

```md
#### Item: <Description> [QA]
```

Rules:

- Exactly one top heading is allowed per file: either `# Feature [<LABEL>]: ...` (hub) or `# Feature-of [<LABEL>]: ...` (child).
- Every Use Case, Story, or Item heading must have exactly one status marker. 
- If a Use Case, Story, or Item heading has no status marker, default to `Spec`.
- During edits, missing labels are normalized by adding `[Spec]`.

### Translation hints in headings (optional)

Translation hints are allowed directly in headings using:

- `[<LANG>: <text>]` (for example `[EN: Coordination Protocol]`)

Placement rule:

- Put translation hints before the status token.
- Example: `### Story: Koordinationsprotokoll [EN: Coordination Protocol] [Spec]`

Notes:

- This does not replace the required status label.
- Use this only for language hints; requirement text remains in the primary document language.

### Semantic hint labels (optional)

Semantic labels can be used to indicate the nature of a requirement in these formats:

- `{name}`
- `{name1, name2, ...}`

Initial examples with intent:

- `{view}`: page/view-level behavior
- `{tab}`: tab-scoped behavior
- `{panel}`: panel/section-level behavior
- `{process}`: process/workflow guidance
- `{function}`: functional behavior or capability
- `{structure}`: structural decomposition or architecture/layout hints
- `{design}`: visual design and UX appearance hints
- `{architecture}`: system/module architecture concerns
- `{data}`: data-handling semantics and default/core seed-data expectations
- `{quality}`: quality targets, testability expectations, and verification criteria
- `{documentation}`: documentation scope, authoring guidance, and manual maintenance requirements
- `{specification}`: specification-system rules, structure, and requirement-model conventions

Extensibility:

- You may introduce additional local labels where useful.

Interpretation:

- Labels are guidance, not hard constraints.
- Example: `{process}` suggests considering the process framework, but does not require using it.

Placement recommendation in headings:

- Put semantic labels directly after the heading title, before translation hints and status.
- Example: `### Story: Koordinationsprotokoll {process} [EN: Coordination Protocol] [Spec]`
- Example with multiple hints: `### Story: Koordinationsprotokoll {design, function} [EN: Coordination Protocol] [Spec]`

### Feature label and file-family convention

Every feature must define a short label (for example `TPLK`).

- Each feature has exactly one feature hub file named `<LABEL>-<Feature-Name>.md`.
- Hub file heading format: `# Feature [<LABEL>]: <Feature-Name>`.
- Immediately under the hub heading, add `## Spezifikationen` and list all subsidiary files there.
- Sub-specification files belonging to this feature use the same label prefix, for example `<LABEL>-Episode-Spezifikation.md`.
- Child file heading format: `# Feature-of [<LABEL>]: <Feature-Name>`.
- The hub file lists all subsidiary files; each child file should reference the hub file.

### Change-marker workflow for implementation handover

To support low-friction changes directly in requirement text, use inline markers in `spec/requirements/*.md`:

- `[[ADD]] ... [[/ADD]]` for inserted text
- `[[REPLACE: "<old snippet>"]] ... [[/REPLACE]]` for targeted replacement
- `[[DELETE: "<old snippet>"]]` for targeted deletion
- `[[NOTE]] ... [[/NOTE]]` for temporary implementation hints

Workflow:

1. Mark the requested changes directly in the specification file.
2. Tell the assistant to implement.
3. The assistant implements code changes from the marked content.
4. The assistant integrates the text seamlessly by resolving markers with minimal wording changes outside marked regions.
5. The assistant removes markers, so the final specification stays clean and readable.

Guardrails:

- Markers may appear at any level (Feature, Use Case, Story, Item, or paragraph text).
- Unaffected specification text should not be rewritten.
- Heading hierarchy and status labels should be preserved unless explicitly requested otherwise.

### Temporary scope markers (for focused assistant work)

When you want the assistant to consider only a specific region in a spec file, you may use temporary delimiters directly in the document:

- `>>>>>` opens the scope to consider/edit
- `<<<<<` closes that scope

Usage notes:

- These delimiters are temporary working marks and are not part of the final requirement text.
- Remove them after the requested implementation/update is completed.
- Combine them with an explicit instruction like "only use content between `>>>>>` and `<<<<<`" for strict scoping.

Status hierarchy gate:

- A parent level cannot be marked `Done` before all lower levels under it are also `Done`.
- Therefore:
 - Use Case `Done` only when all nested Stories/Items are `Done`.
 - Story `Done` only when all nested Items are `Done`.

Starting-status policy during updates:

- Default assumption is `Spec`.
- Exception: when requirements are reverse-engineered from existing implementation code, starting status is `Impl`.

TODO marker for open discussion:

- The inline text label `TODO` is allowed in specification documents.
- Anything after `TODO` on the same textual line is treated as still to be discussed (not yet finalized requirement text).
- If the TODO sentence wraps into visual overflow lines, those wrapped continuations belong to the same TODO statement.

## How Specs Map To Test Pipelines

Specs are consumed by tooling in `qa/spec_tools/`, which supports:

- `spec-case` -> spec-derived HTTP checks
- `partner-case` -> partner-driven UI + DB scenarios

Operational commands are documented in `doc/test-manual.qmd`.

## Writing New Specs

Use fenced blocks with JSON payload:

```md
```spec-case
{
  "id": "example-id",
  "scope": "server",
  "name": "Short human-readable title",
  "request": { "method": "GET", "path": "/api/health" },
  "expect": { "status": 200, "json_subset": { "status": "ok" } }
}
```
```

Supported scope values:

- `server`
- `client_server`

For complex user-workflow scenarios that include UI interaction and DB verification, use `partner-case` blocks and execute them via `python -m qa.spec_tools.run_partner_specs`.

## Grammar of JSON Sections (JSPON/JSON)

The parser reads fenced blocks with either:

- ```` ```spec-case ```` for standard HTTP checks
- ```` ```partner-case ```` for richer scenario checks
- ```` ```conceptual-case ```` for conceptual/domain consistency checks

Inside the fence, the content must be valid JSON (double quotes, no trailing commas).

### `spec-case` shape

```json
{
  "id": "string-unique-id",
  "scope": "server | client_server",
  "name": "human readable title",
  "request": {
    "method": "GET | POST | PATCH | DELETE | ...",
    "path": "/api/..."
  },
  "expect": {
    "status": 200,
    "json_subset": { "optional": "partial JSON match" },
    "body_contains": ["optional text fragment"]
  }
}
```

Required fields:

- `id`, `scope`, `request.method`, `request.path`, `expect.status`

### `partner-case` shape

`partner-case` is for workflow-style scenarios (UI + DB checks). Keep it explicit and deterministic:

- Scenario identity: `id`, `scope`, `name`
- UI intent and actions (for example `ui_flow`)
- Verifications (for example `verify.ui_...`, `verify.database_...`)

Tip: copy an existing partner case and adapt values incrementally.

### `conceptual-case` shape

`conceptual-case` is for conceptual architecture/domain consistency checks.

```json
{
  "id": "domain-database-consistency",
  "name": "Conceptual model and SQLAlchemy model stay aligned",
  "checker": "domain_database_sync",
  "diagram": "spec/domain/gen-domain.puml",
  "mapping": "spec/domain/domain-sync-mapping.json"
}
```

Required fields:

- always required: `id`, `name`, `checker`
- checker-specific:
  - `domain_database_sync`: `diagram`, `mapping`
  - `architecture_dependency_sync`: `rules`

### Important note on key stability vs flexibility

- For `spec-case`, keys are effectively **stable** because they are parsed into generated tests (`id`, `scope`, `request`, `expect`, etc.).
- For `partner-case`, keys like `open_recipients_view` and `database_contains_created_patient` are currently **conventions** (human-readable contract), not yet a strict machine-enforced schema.
- For `conceptual-case`, keys are effectively **stable** because they are parsed by `qa.spec_tools.run_conceptual_specs`.

So:

- `open_recipients_view` is a recommended, readable key (not globally reserved yet).
- `database_contains_created_patient` is also a recommended verification key (not globally reserved yet).

Best practice for now: keep these names stable across files for consistency, even though the parser does not strictly enforce them yet.

## Writing JSON By Hand (Practical Hints)

- Start from a known-good template and edit one field at a time.
- Keep `id` stable and unique (kebab-case works well).
- Use only double quotes in JSON keys/values.
- Validate from outside in:
  1) braces/brackets match,
  2) commas are correct,
  3) required fields are present.
- Prefer deterministic values:
  - stable endpoints
  - stable expected status
  - avoid fragile text unless necessary
- Keep one behavior per block; split big scenarios into multiple cases.

## How I Can Help From Our Dialogue

From your plain-language requirements, the AI assistant can:

1. Draft a first `spec-case` or `partner-case` block in Markdown,
2. Place it in the correct `spec/...` folder,
3. Generate/adjust executable tests,
4. Run the pipelines and interpret failures,
5. Produce a step-by-step improvement plan and execute fixes with you.

A good prompt style is:

- "Create a spec for X, precondition Y, expected result Z."
- "Turn this flow into a partner-case and run it."
- "Update the spec after this behavior change and rerun tests."

## How Test-Code Generation Works (In Principle)

At a high level:

1. The parser scans Markdown files in `spec/` for fenced blocks.
2. `spec-case` JSON blocks are parsed and normalized into internal case objects.
3. The generator writes executable Python `unittest` files into `qa/tests/generated/`.
4. The runner executes those tests and writes a Markdown report with suggestions.

For partner scenarios:

- `run_partner_specs.py` currently executes the Playwright scenario suite in `frontend/test/` and then performs DB verification.
- This is an intentionally flexible partner-assisted setup: the `partner-case` text guides scenario intent, while executable scenario logic currently lives in maintained Playwright tests.

For conceptual scenarios:

- `run_conceptual_specs.py` parses `spec/testing/conceptual/*.md` for `conceptual-case` blocks.
- The `domain_database_sync` checker compares conceptual domain spec (`spec/domain/*.puml`) against SQLAlchemy model graph and writes `qa/reports/latest-conceptual-report.md`.
- The `architecture_dependency_sync` checker validates backend dependencies against `spec/architecture/dependency-rules.json` and also reports via `qa/reports/latest-conceptual-report.md`.

## About Playwright Code Generation

There are two different things to distinguish:

1. **Playwright test execution** (what this repo does now): runs existing test files from `frontend/test/`.
2. **Playwright codegen/recorder** (optional): records browser actions and emits starter test code.

Key points:

- Playwright itself is **rule-based tooling**, not an AI model.
- Playwright codegen is generally **deterministic for a given interaction sequence**, but output can still vary when UI structure/selectors change.
- Playwright codegen runs **locally** on your machine and does **not require remote AI requests**.

In this project:

- The current pipeline does not auto-call Playwright codegen.
- Test code is maintained in-repo (`frontend/test/`) and can be authored manually or with assistant help.
- If AI assistant help is used to write or refactor tests, whether that assistant is local or remote depends on the assistant platform configuration (separate from Playwright itself).

## Copy-Paste Templates

### Template: `spec-case`

```md
```spec-case
{
  "id": "server-example-id",
  "scope": "server",
  "name": "Describe expected behavior",
  "request": {
    "method": "GET",
    "path": "/api/health"
  },
  "expect": {
    "status": 200,
    "json_subset": {
      "status": "ok"
    },
    "body_contains": [
      "ok"
    ]
  }
}
```
```

### Template: `partner-case`

```md
```partner-case
{
  "id": "partner-example-id",
  "scope": "client_server_partner",
  "name": "Describe end-to-end user workflow",
  "ui_flow": {
    "login_ext_id": "TKOORD",
    "open_recipients_view": true,
    "create_patient": {
      "pid_prefix": "AUTO",
      "first_name": "Spec",
      "name": "Patient",
      "date_of_birth": "1990-01-01"
    }
  },
  "verify": {
    "ui_contains_created_pid": true,
    "database_contains_created_patient": true
  }
}
```
```

## Authoring Guidelines

- Keep one business behavior per `id`.
- Use stable expectations (avoid brittle UI text unless required).
- Prefer deterministic data and explicit preconditions.
- For workflow cases, capture user intent and verification criteria (`ui` and `db`) clearly.

## Relationship To Other Manuals

- Server startup and env config: `doc/server-manual.qmd`
- Client startup and dev runtime: `doc/client-manual.qmd`
- Seed profiles and data categories: `doc/seeding-manual.qmd`
- Test workflow operations: `doc/test-manual.qmd`
- Architecture review operations: `doc/architecture-review-manual.qmd`
- Shared architecture vocabulary: `doc/architecture-glossary.qmd`
